In this paper, we focus on one important usability-  a
related issue, transaction fee estimation in the Bitcoin  t
blockchain system. The transaction fee serves as an in-  a
centive to complete the transfer of Bitcoin assets (recorded h
in a transaction) on the blockchain [8,19,20,10]. Gen-  m
erally, a transaction with a higher fee is likely to be  n
processed faster, whereas a transaction with a lower  c
fee or no fee may be delayed for long or may never  m
be processed. However, a user is required to specify a  c
transaction fee when the user submits the transaction.  c
If the specified fee is too high, the user will suffer from  k
over-spending; if the specified fee is too low, the trans-  m
action may not be confirmed in time. Unfortunately, a  t
user usually has no idea about how to properly preset a  h
fee. We strongly believe the problem needs an in-depth  S
study. A helpful fee estimation software could attract a  n
large number of users and win a substantial portion of  o
the market.  c
Most of the existing fee estimation tools were de-  i
veloped as built-in components of Bitcoin software by  o

during the confirmation process. BtcFlow returns the  p

rival rate is equal to or less than the confirmation rate.  m

Although the existing industry tools have provided  t

Other factors
nclude block capacity (as it controls the upper bound
f the transaction volume in each block), the blockchain
etwork (such as computational power) evolving mining
fficiency (which in return affects the speed of transac-
ion confirmation), etc.
Other than the industry products, AI-Shehabi  [1]
roposed a neural network framework to model  the
nteractions of transaction fee and transaction confir-
ation time. By assuming that the unconfirmed trans-
ctions in the mempool comprise the future block se-
uence in the blockchain,  it organises these transac-
ions, according to their transaction fees,  into a vir-
ual sequence of blocks. It then uses neural networks
o extract patterns among the transaction fee, the vir-
ual confirmation time (virtual block position), and its
ctual confirmation time, which are then used for fee
rediction.
To further investigate the transaction fee estima-
ion problem, we construct a new framework integrating
ore details in transactions and blocks. In our frame-
ork, we take into account, the features of the trans-
ction itself, unconfirmed transactions in the mempool
nd the blockchain confirmation environment, such as
he mining rate, block capacity, etc. We tackle this
roblem by leveraging the power of neural networks.
pecifically, we make the following contributions
– We organize and systematically document the prin-
ciple and the detailed estimation procedure of exist-
ing transaction fee estimation works from industry
tools. Meanwhile, we analyse the limitations of these
works. Finally, we implemented them to support of-
fline evaluation..

ransaction feerate measures the fee density of a trans-

ime only a limited number of transactions can be con-
rmed, hence miners would prioritise transactions with
igher feeratess in order to increase mining profits.
Transactions can be submitted to the blockchain
etwork via the Bitcoin wallets (desktop wallet, mobile
allet, web wallet, paper wallet, etc.) [24]. The sub-
itted pending transactions are then broadcast across
arious nodes. If they meet the transactions’ validity
riteria [2], Bitcoin nodes will add them to their mem-
ools (memory pools), where transactions wait until
hey can be included (mined) into a block. Miners are
he nodes responsible for the verification and block con-
truction. Specifically, miners first select unconfirmed
ransactions from the mempool to construct their own
andidate blocks, and then compete to determine a
omputational solution (Proof of Work (Pow)) to at-
ach their candidature block to the blockchain (referred
o as ‘mining a block’). Among all the miners, only the
rst miner who solves this computational issue will be
ewarded with a fixed Bitcoin reward from the blockchain
ystem plus transaction fees from the chosen transac-
ions. Once a new block is added to the blockchain,
hese transactions are confirmed and will then be re-
oved from the miner’s mempool.
A transaction is complete when it is recorded in a
lockchain block. The block stores information about
he selected transactions, as well as information about
he mining complexity, block size, etc. In the meantime,
he Bitcoin blockchain system keeps track of the gener-
tion time of each block.

BtcFlow  infers the transaction fee based on analysing
the rate of transaction entering the mempool and trans-
action confirmation leaving the mempool within a given
time interval. Specifically, it models the blockchain mem-
pool as a container with an inflow pouring in (trans-
action submission) and an outflow flowing out at at
a specified rate (transaction confirmation is assumed
following a specific poisson distribution). The ultimate

Limeng Zhang et al.
im is to empty the container within the given time
the mempool is emptied with no unconfirmed transac-
ions left). During the procedure, the estimated trans-
ction feerate serves as a valve to regulate the input
ow so that the container can be emptied within the
iven time.
In terms of data sources, the future inflow is sim-
lated using the confirmed transactions in the Bitcoin
lockchain, the outflow is calculated through a speci-
ed function, and the container begins with the uncon-
rmed transactions in the current mempool. Thus, the
eneral estimation procedure of BtcFlow can be formu-
ated as:
ˆ = BtcFlow(tˆ, Tm, {T}, ϑ, ConstBtc)
ith ConstBtc = {BLOCK, p, hn}, where hn refers to
urrent block height, p ∈ [0, 1] is a predefined parameter
sed to set the block production rate, whereas BLOCK
pecifies the capacity of transactions that can be con-
rmed in a single block. Given a p and a BLOCK, the
utflow within the given time interval ϑ is then calcu-
ated.
.1 Estimation procedure
tcFlow works under the assumption that transaction
onfirmation follows the rules that higher feerate trans-
ctions always have priority to be confirmed earlier
han the lower ones.  It means that when estimating
he transaction fee, BtcFlow only needs to focus on es-
imating the minimum boundary feerate transactions
hich can ensure the unconfirmed transactions with
igher feerates ( equal to or larger than the minimum
ee rate) can be drained within the given time. The esti-
ating process can be divided into two parts: The mod-
lling process, which is designed to analyse the inflow
tream, current container state, and the outflow stream
f all feerate-range transactions in the given expected
ime interval, and The estimation process, which is used
o generate the target transaction feerate based on the
imulated data streams. The process is illustrated in
ig. 1 (and Algorithm 1).
– Modeling process. In this process, BtcFlow mon-
itor three data streams of transactions in terms of
their transaction feerates: (1) the submitted trans-
actions entering the mempool (the inflow), (2) the
unconfirmed transactions in the current mempool
(the current container state), and (3) the confirmed
transactions leaving the mempool (the outflow).
Considering that the value of feerate is continuous,
it  is  impractical  to compare the transaction flow
at each feerate scale.

BCore  is what the community calls the codebase used
to control  the currency.  It gives an estimate of  the
transaction feerate for a given block interval. Similar
to BtcFlow, BCore seeks out the lowest feerate to en-
sure that transactions are confirmed within the spec-
ified timeframe. The distinction is that BCore pays
more attention to historical transactions that have been
recorded in the blockchain. It argues that by combin-
ing the feerates of prior transactions confirmed within
the same block interval, an expected feerate may be de-
termined. Meanwhile, when computing the estimation,
BCore considers the records in recent blocks to be more
useful than those in previous blocks.
This prediction procedure can be formulated based
on its data resources:
rˆ = BCore(Tm, {T}, tˆ, θ, ConstBCore)
where ConstBCore = {α, hn, p1, p2}, where α is a decay
parameter α ∈ (0, 1] used to scale the effect of different
blocks. The impact of previous blocks will  fade by a
fraction of α when a new block is mined. p1 and p2 are  d

7
sed to control the estimation procedure. p1 is used to
nsure that enough samples have been obtained, while
2 is used to manage the percentage of successfully con-
rmed transactions. For training, BCore defaults to us-
ng all historical confirmed transactions prior to the cur-
ent block height hn.
.1 Estimation procedure
he estimation procedure illustrated in Fig. 2 (more
etails  in Algorithm 2)  is composed of two phrases:
he preProcessing process in BCore is to collect trans-
ction confirmation related to the transaction feerate.
o be specific, BCore focuses on three parts of infor-
ation when given a specific feerate and an expected
onfirmation time: overall historical confirmation in the
ntire blockchain, transaction confirmation related to
his  feerate and unconfirmed transactions that have
een waiting longer than the given confirmation time.
Core returns the estimated feerate based on transac-
ion records that meets the model requirements during
he estimation procedure.
– PreProcessing process. When dealing with the
historical confirmation information, BCore believes
that recent blocks can provide more accurate in-
formation than older blocks. The influence is pre-
cisely described as an exponentially weighted mov-
ing trend. When a new block is formed,  the old
9
blocks’ impact fades at a set rate . Considering that

text:
machine learning (MSLP)
MSLP [1] transforms the transaction fee problem into a
classic binary classification problem, predicting whether
a transaction can be confirmed within a specified time-
frame based on its feerate. To be specific, MSLP first

9
alculates an estimated confirmation time based on the
empool. The estimated confirmation time is then com-
ared to the historical confirmation time in the blockchain.
f the estimated confirmation time exceeds the recorded
onfirmation time,  it means that this transaction can
e confirmed earlier under this feerate. In other words,
his charge assures that the transaction will be com-
leted within the estimated confirmation time. Finally,
SLP uses the capabilities of a neural network to learn
he confirmation result (success or failure). It focuses on
ransaction priority information in the mempool, as op-
osed to BtcFlow, which focuses on transaction feerate
ow change in the mempool.
The prediction procedure can be formulated based
n its data resources:
ˆ = MSLP(Tm, {T}, tˆ, θ, ConstMSLP )
ith ConstMSLP = {BLOCK, SLICE, hn}. When MSLP
imulates the confirmation time of a transaction in the
empool, the capacity of each block is assumed to be
LOCK. Similarly, each block is broken into smaller
hunks of SLICE size. By default, transactions con-
rmed in the block height anterior to the current block
eight hn are used in the training process.
.1 Estimation procedure
he estimation procedure as shown in Fig. 3 (and Al-
orithm 3) is composed of two phases: the training pro-
ess and the estimation process. The training process
s used to discover the inherent patterns among trans-
ction confirmation time, current memepool, and the
ransaction feerate. In the estimation process, given the
onfirmation time, MSLP will return the transaction
eerate to ensure confirmation based on the learnt pat-
erns in the training process.
– Training process. In the mempool, unconfirmed
transactions compete for confirmation in the blockchain.
To highlight this competitiveness, MSLP coined the
term ‘expectation confirmation time’ to represent
the transaction processing priority. The unconfirmed
transaction is treated by MLSP as a queue sorted
by transaction feerate, and only a block of trans-
actions (related to the fixed capacity of a block in
the Bitcoin blockchain system) in this queue can be
confirmed at a time. As a result, the expected confir-
mation time of a transaction is proportional to the
number of blocks in the queue before it is processed.
Meanwhile, certain other unseen elements may have
an impact on the confirmation procedure. By train-
ing the model with history confirmation instances,

ue to the low block capacity, the majority of sub-
itted transactions may experience various confirma-
ion delays. Transactions are selected and added to the
iner’s mempool after submission, where they com-
ete for confirmation in the next block. A transaction
s considered complete when it is recorded in a block
n the blockchain. In the confirmation process, trans-
ction fees are considered as an incentive to confirm
ransactions into the blockchain. To sum up, we sum-
arize three groups of features that may influence the
ransaction confirmation:
– Transaction features, which describes the sub-
mitted transaction.
– Mempool states, which records the distribution
of feerates of unconfirmed transactions in the mem-
pool,  implicitly modelling the competition among
unconfirmed transactions.
– Network features, which reflects the characteris-
tics of the mined blocks including block size, block
generation speed, etc.
These three groups of  features correspond to the
hree types of information fed to the estimation func-
ion F in Section 3. Although transaction features are
lready available in the submitted transaction, network
eatures and mempool states are not known. However,
uch features are desirable, because if we had known
ow many transactions would be contained in future
locks, how fast future blocks would be generated, how
ompetitive the submitted transaction would be in fu-
ure mempools, we would increase the chance to predict
he confirmation fee more accurately. Consequently, in
ENN, our main idea is to predict network features
nd mempool states from historical state sequences by
tilizing sequence learning models. Finally, we combine
he three groups of features to do the estimation.
The prediction procedure can be formulated based
n its data resources:

ions confirmed previous to current block height hn are
sed in the training process.
.1 Estimation procedure
he estimation framework can be divided into two lay-
rs, one feature extraction layer  to extract patterns

12
from network features, mempool states and the sub-
mitted transaction itself, and one prediction layer to
analyze the relationship between transaction fee and
the extracted features. Fig. 4 shows the framework.
7.1.1 Feature extraction layer
It includes three parts. Other than modelling the sub-
mitted transaction itself, the feature extraction layer
also predicts the future characteristics of block states
and model mempool competition states of the uncon-
firmed transactions.
1. Transaction features contain information on the trans-
action that has been submitted and is awaiting con-
firmation. We pick features that we believe may
affect a transaction’s validation and confirmation.
The transaction vector contains:
– number of inputs, number of outputs Miners need
to seek for the source transactions pointed to
the new transaction’s  inputs when confirming
a transaction, which means that the number of
transaction inputs and outputs affects the veri-
fication complexity.
– transaction version, transaction size and weight
We use both transaction size of raw data and
transaction weight to characterize transactions.
– transaction first seen time, confirmation times-
tamp and confirmation block height. The first-
seen time refers to the time that a transaction is
first observed. Because it’s difficult to determine
the precise submission time of a historical trans-
action, we use the publicly available first-seen
time.
2. Current mempool  states  indicate the transactions
feerate distribution in the mempool. Inspired by ‘bucket’
term in BCore, we classify our transactions in the
mempool into different feerate buckets and make use
of the transaction count in each bucket u to model
mempool states Tm. The bucket interval is the same
as BCore with 5% increasing interval for accuracy.

3. Network features are expected to encode future block
size and generation speed, which can affect a trans-
action’s confirmation time. Historical network fea-
tures are learned as a sequence to predict future
network features.
– block size, block weight and transaction count
We use three factors to characterise the size of
a block, namely, the overall size of transactions
(Bytes), the overall weight of transactions (Weight)
and the transaction count in a block.

Bitcoin system, which is tuned to maintain an
average 10-minute block frequency.
– block time The mining time of this block. It re-
veals the block generation speed.
– average feerate in block The average feerate of all
the transactions in the block. This indicator is
designed to reveal the feerate trend in continuous
blocks.

Approach 1: LSTM [16] extract patterns by aggre-
gating information on a token-by-token basis in
a sequential order and summarizes the sequence
into a context vector. To be specific, at each time
step, LSTM maintains a hidden vector h and a
memory vector c responsible for state updates
and output prediction [18], and the final state is
used as the extracted patterns from the sequence

uate the predictive error. Higher feerate transactions
tend to confirm earlier than lower feerate transactions,
hence in the fee estimate problem, the lower bound fee is
usually returned. Compared to MAPE, RMSE concen-
trates more on avoiding high abnormal values, i.e. ab-
normal transaction fee values, which are outliers, have
high impact on the error values.

– MSLP: It is a one-layer neural network with a lin-

– FENN variants: It includes LSTM models, atten-

positions.

Conclusion
his work begins by documenting and analyzing previ-
us transaction fee estimation research. Then we pro-
osed a new neural network-based framework to ana-
yze complex interactions from a wider range of sources,
ncluding transaction details, network features, and mem-
ool states, in order to address the issues of inferior es-
imation accuracy and limited knowledge used in pre-
ious work. The effectiveness and efficiency of our sug-
ested architecture have been demonstrated on genuine
lockchain datasets.
